one_line_takeaway: A dedicated, SFT-trained filter model that sanitizes untrusted data before it reaches a black-box LLM cuts prompt-injection ASR from >40% to ~2% with ~1-2% utility loss and plug-and-play deployment, but still falls to strong optimization-based adaptive attacks (83% ASR).
key_finding: DataFilter drives prompt-injection ASR to near zero while keeping utility within ~2 points of the undefended model, achieving a better security-utility trade-off than all tested model-agnostic baselines. Averaged across benchmarks it cuts ASR from >40% to ~2% at ~1% utility cost. Its best model-agnostic competitor, PromptArmor, is beaten on both axes (avg ASR 2.2% vs 5.9%; avg utility drop 1.0% vs 4.1%), and it is far more secure than sandwich prompting (2.2% vs 22.8% avg ASR). It generalizes from single-turn Alpaca training to multi-turn agentic tool-calling benchmarks and to unseen attack templates. Crucially, the paper is honest that it does NOT fully solve the problem: under a strong optimization-based / RL-genetic adaptive attack (ref [12]) it is still the best defense but ASR remains 83% (vs 100% undefended), confirming it is a practical short/medium-term defense, not a guaranteed one.
core_contribution: DataFilter, a test-time, model-agnostic defense against indirect prompt injection. Instead of hardening the backend LLM, the authors interpose a separately-trained small filter model that takes both the trusted user instruction u and the untrusted data x, strips out injected/extraneous instructions, and passes only sanitized data x_clean to the backend LLM. The filter is a Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model fine-tuned via SFT on simulated injections. The paper's central claim is that this is the first model-agnostic defense to simultaneously deliver strong security AND preserve utility for black-box commercial LLMs, combining the deploy-anywhere property of system-level defenses with the low utility cost of well-trained fine-tuning defenses, but without needing the backend model's weights. Note: PromptArmor and PromptLocate are cited as concurrent filtering work, not the authors' contribution; the paper differentiates by fine-tuning a dedicated filter rather than querying an external API detector.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against LLM-integrated agents. The system input is split into a trusted prompt/instruction (set by the developer) and untrusted data (retrieved documents, tool-call returns, website HTML, emails). An attacker injects an instruction into the data to override the user's task and steer the agent toward attacker-chosen actions (e.g., data exfiltration, malicious API/tool calls). The attacker is assumed to have full knowledge of the benign instruction and the LLM prompt template but cannot modify them. Attack succeeds if the LLM treats the injected text as an instruction to follow rather than as data. The paper explicitly EXCLUDES optimization-based attacks (e.g., GCG variants) from the primary threat scope, arguing they are hard to mount against closed models, though it later tests such adaptive attacks and concedes they break the defense.
limitations: (1) Adds inference overhead: the filter must run on every batch of new untrusted data (cost <$0.0005 and latency <0.60s per sample, but +17.5% wall-clock on the fast GPT-4o setting). (2) Broken by strong optimization/RL-based adaptive attacks (ref [12]); ASR only drops to 83% on AgentDojo under that worst-case attack. (3) Struggles with very long benign user prompts, so developers must extract and pass a concise user instruction to the filter (e.g., InjecAgent's user message bundles tool docs/policies and degrades the filter) - i.e., some non-zero integration effort. (4) Documented false negatives when the injection is semantically indistinguishable from the legitimate task (e.g., a financial-transfer injection inside a bill the user asked to pay) and false positives on benign imperative content (recipe cooking steps). (5) A prompt-less filter variant achieved 0% ASR but destroyed utility, showing the design depends on conditioning on the user instruction. (6) Evaluations use fixed backends (mainly gpt-4o and Llama-3.1-8B); non-determinism of gpt-4o even at temperature 0 adds result variance.

datasets_used: Alpaca (training; all N=19K samples with non-empty data part; Cleaned-Alpaca variant referenced)
SEP (security + instruction-following; 9.1K samples, 1K randomly sampled for eval)
InjecAgent (security; 1K API-calling samples; no utility measure)
AgentDojo (security + utility; 97 tasks / 949 instances, multi-tool agent)
AlpacaEval2 (utility; 805 samples, 208 with non-empty data; length-controlled WinRate vs gpt4_1106_preview)
quantitative_results: Averaged across benchmarks: ASR reduced from >40% to ~2%; utility reduced by ~1%
vs PromptArmor: avg ASR 2.2% vs 5.9%, avg utility drop 1.0% vs 4.1%; vs sandwich: avg ASR 2.2% vs 22.8%

AgentDojo ASR (gpt-4o, DataFilter): Direct 1.2%, Ignore-Previous 0.1%, Important Instructions 0.2%, Tool Knowledge 0.0% (avg 0.4%, max 1.2%) vs None 3.1/3.2/42.2/42.5% and PromptArmor avg 0.7% / max 2.5%
InjecAgent ASR (DataFilter): gpt-4o Base 2.0% / Enhanced 0.0%; Llama-3.1-8B Base 2.1% / Enhanced 1.2% vs None gpt-4o 34.4/38.6% and Llama 23.1/37.8%
SEP ASR (gpt-4o, DataFilter across 6 attacks): 3.4/1.5/1.8/1.4/2.4/2.2%; (Llama-3.1-8B): 2.4/2.5/4.6/3.5/3.9/2.6% vs None Llama up to 95% (Completion) and 82-92% on other completion/context attacks
AgentDojo benign utility (gpt-4o): DataFilter 79.4% vs None 81.4% (~2% drop); DataSentinel collapses to 36.6%
AlpacaEval2 WinRate: DataFilter gpt-4o 54.1% (None 54.0%), Llama-3.1-8B 26.2% (None 25.9%) - essentially no utility loss
Adaptive human-designed attacks (Table VII): AgentDojo 15.7%->0.0%, SEP gpt-4o 72.2%->1.0%, SEP Llama 77.6%->0.3% with DataFilter
Adaptive LLM/RL-based attack on AgentDojo (Table VIII): DataFilter ASR 83% (lowest; None 100%, PromptArmor 93%, PromptGuard 94%), utility 76.3% (highest)
Overhead (Table IX): GPT-5.1 +3.7% cost / +4.0% time; GPT-4o +1.0% cost / +17.5% time; <$0.0005 and <0.60s added per sample
